speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. Would you continue to tell us, when you were in Wulkow, what your duties were there, and what the living conditions were like in that labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were assigned to build, and we did, 32 pre-fab barracks for the German high command. So they couldn't stay in Berlin. Berlin was bombed. And this was in a forest, so we cut out the trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you? Where was it located? What part of Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in East Germany. I-- I-- I couldn't put my finger on. It wasn't around the Order-- Order-- Oder River. And I don't know how far it was from Berlin. Anyway, high command was there. We build these barracks. And-- and we built, at one time, we were digging a ditch close to the highway. And six of us were digging the-- a big ditch. And a-- a German lady was passing by on the highway. And it was close by to where we were working. And a German guard was guarding us. And then we didn't have-- I think we didn't have the shirts on. And then-- and the lady said to her child, holding in her arms, [SPEAKING GERMAN], make so to the-- to the men there. And one of us said, heh, you wouldn't do that if you knew who we are. And she said, don't worry about that. It's just, remember these guys, when the war is over. Phew. So that was-- she was on our side. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: What were we doing in? We were building these barracks. And towards the end, I told you, and the-- the Russian army was coming, who liberated us. And we had to evacuate and get into the train, six kilometers to [INAUDIBLE]. That was the town where we-- that was the town for Theresienstadt. Theresienstadt didn't have a railway station. And here, they put us into these-- these cattle cars and took us for 11 days through Germany, back to Theresienstadt. We-- What was the life like in the cars? Describe what--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the train?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Train. Well, we didn't have food. There was just a pail for-- we were sitting on some elevation on boards. And sort of life was ebbing out. And we were just waiting for-- for the end. So we were pretty relaxed because you haven't go the strength-- there's no food in your tummy. And you haven't got the strength to fight anything. So you just waited for the end. And then, at one station, they took us out. And we couldn't-- when we got out, we couldn't recognize each other, we were so dilapidated and the faces were all different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine, after certain days when you don't eat, you lose so much weight. And the people say, who are you? Are you [? Rusiska? ?] No way. You know? That was a fellow who was with-- with us there. And when we arrive in Theresienstadt, there were no men there, only women. And these women came to the barracks where we on the third floor. And they yelled up to us, hey, did you see my dad? Did you see my husband? Did you see my brother? We didn't see anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: We then said, you-- you hungry. Send us some food. So they brought a-- a-- a little basket with food in. And on the ropes, we got the food up. We started to eat, but we couldn't hold the food because they-- we got diarrhea. We ran to the washroom and couldn't hold the food. We were just going down, down, down. You know, after so many days, you don't eat, you start eating, you can't hold the food. We got out, and I remember one situation, when it was already getting better, and many people who were on the march from these camps, dilapidated, they were going through Theresienstadt. And as I was standing there, there was one of the-- one of these detachments of these people in-- in the concentration uniforms.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one guy was yelling at me, Albert, Albert? You recognize me. I said, no. Who are you? I'm Paul Foder. I'm from [? Levice. ?] Please, save me, save me. So I followed them where they were taken. And I said, I've got to save this boy. So I took all the bread I had. I took the bread to this place, to this place where-- in the-- in the-- in the-- in the barracks were these people were kept.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got there, and I was handing him the bread. And you-- I've never seen anything like that. All these people started to fight for the loaf of bread. And they scratched each other. They were like wild animals. I've never seen, in my life, anything like that. And then, a few days later, I came to look for him. And he was-- I was told that he has typhus. And he was lying there in the room with other people on the floor. And he was just dying of hunger. And that was the end of Paul Foder.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you recall from that time in Theresienstadt? How many people were left in the camp at that--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just about 11,000 women and us 200 men. And all the men, and even the-- the-- the government, the Jewish government, in big transport, 5,000 of them were shipped to-- to-- to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, well, I was-- I was in-- I was in Wulkow. I wasn't there. But my-- my future wife's parents, my future father-in-law, was in the government. He was like a-- a messenger there. And they overtaken there. And they kept these 5,000 people there isolated in Auschwitz. And they called it a family camp. And on I think-- I think, on October 28-- that's when Czechoslovakia was-- was-- was born, in 19-- in 1918. Czechoslovakia, October 28 was born. Or was it March the 7th, when the first president, Tomas Garrigue Masaryk, was his birthday. So, on one of these days, they gassed all these 5,000 people. And I remember-- I-- I wasn't there, you know. But then I read a book about it. So there was a chap in Theresienstadt who was a German Jew. He was an athlete. His name was Fredy Hirsch. He was a handsome man, an athlete. Beautiful body. He was in charge of the youth in Theresienstadt. And he was with these 5,000 people. And he was asked to start a revolution of these 5,000 people. And he accepted it. And on one night, he-- he couldn't take the responsibility. And he always wore a-- a chain on his neck, with a Magen David-- with the-- with a Mezuzah in there. And he opened the Mezuzah, it was poison in there. And that night, he poisoned himself. And that was the end of the uprising of these 5,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I read in the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Were there any uprisings, or any revolts of any kind in Theresienstadt? Anything that you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There was one case, in the very beginning, before I came. First, the-- the chief, Mr.-- [SIGHS] Mr.-- [INAUDIBLE]. He was named by the Germans to be the chief of Theresienstadt, of the whole ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was-- he's taken 300 men, called AK, young men, to prepare the ghetto there. And they started to revolt. Look, what did I take-- what did you take us here for? There's nothing here. And he took this chief-- I-- [INAUDIBLE]. Forgot his name. He took them to a barrack, to the second floor, and they were revolting there. We want to go back. Why did I take-- why did you take us before? You said this is-- we'd be working here. There's nothing here. And he went to the windows, opened the windows. He said, OK. Anybody who wants to die, jump now.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from that on, everybody was silent. But he was with these 5,000 people. He died. He was the chief of them, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you returned to Theresienstadt, when did you get back, what-- what was the date that you returned?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date was '45-- February '45-- February 14, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the living conditions like at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had food. We were, those 200, you know, we were well fed after we got to our-- you know, to our-- get our bodies back. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind-- what was the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you fed? What were you fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fed?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember. There was pretty good food then. So we-- there was only 11,000 women there and us 200 men. And '45, in May, we were liberated by the Russian army. And the Russian tanks rolled in. And we tore down the barbed wire. And it was pandemonium broke out there because the Germans were-- the gestapo were running, throwing away their arms, and their jackets, and insignias. They were running for life, you know. And the tanks were rolling in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a-- a encounter with a-- standing there and thinking, where do we go from here? And a-- a Russian soldier came to me. And he said-- he said in Russian, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], are you a Jew? I said, [RUSSIAN], I am Jew. He said [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], I am also Jew. He said [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]? What's your name? I told him my Hebrew name. He told me his Hebrew name. From Odessa, he is. So we embraced. I said thank you they-- for liberating us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What-- how-- after liberation, what happened in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Russians took over. And as I told you, that time I met Ingeborg Porges in '46. She was carrying a little suitcase. And she was chubby. She [? our ?] ladies didn't have-- didn't menstruate, the girls there. [INAUDIBLE] The food was putting on weight from.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, can I carry that little suitcase for you? She said sure. I carried the suitcase. This was in [INAUDIBLE] already in-- in the train station. And she came on, without a peep on a wagon, an open wagon, and she was carrying that to the station, to the suitcase that took it over. And we waited for the train. We got into the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were sitting on the suitcase in the train. And I had a little briefcase. And the Russians gave us margarine as a food. You know, and I had the margarine in the suitcase. And I had it in the suitcase. And my arm was on it. And the margarine melted away and made a-- a-- a spot on the leather case, and on my shirt. And when we arrived in Prague, I saw the problem. She got out first. And I said, I can't go out like this, you know, with all this grease. And I-- so I stayed there, and she didn't wait for me. She walked away. So I lost her. When I went to the to the registry office, everybody had to be registered in Prague. I found out. I knew her name, Ingeborg Porges. And said, have you got an Ingeborg Porges. Yes. She stays with her sister at nine-- number nine [INAUDIBLE]. And a friend, he came with me. We went to [INAUDIBLE] number nine. I rang the bell. Her sister [? Vera ?] came to the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: She opened the door. Is Inge here? Can I speak to Inge? I met here in Theresienstadt. She looked at me and said, OK, hold on. Stay where you are. I'll ask Inge to see you. She came in her-- in her house coat. She looked at me, bewildered, you know, to see me. And, OK, come in. We're sitting in the room, and talked, and that's how it started. How long did you stay in Theresienstadt after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After?

speaker: interviewer
text: After you were liberated. How long did you stay in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: From February to May the 5th, or 6th, or 7th, or-- I don't remember the day. I think it was the 5th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the camp governed at that point? How many people were in the-- remained in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: After liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation, all these 11,000 women, us 200 men, as far as I can remember, were under Russians. And they gave us [RUSSIAN], a piece of paper, that we were liberated. We can go anywhere. We are free to go. And they gave us food, and the fed us, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed for-- from February to May. When did leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this-- yeah. I came back from Wulkow February, and the war was still on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated? And you liberated in May?

speaker: interviewee
text: May 1945, by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long after liberation did you leave? Did you leave immediately? When you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- yeah, well yeah. I didn't want, there was-- I was-- I was friendly with the young lady. Or not intimate, but friendly. And her husband wasn't there. And she-- she didn't know where her husband is. He didn't came back. He didn't come back. Her father was a-- he was called Baron [? Friedman. ?] He owned a lot of land in Prague. He was well-known in Prague. And in Theresienstadt, they needed him because he was a farmer and knew a lot about farmings, farming. And Theresienstadt, a lot of farms around, which had food, you know, grown food there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was in charge as Mr.-- Mr.-- [? Mr. Friedman. ?] And his daughter, this [? Vera, ?] told me, oh, my father's chauffeur is coming to pick us up. Would I come along? I said sure. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- yes, but how did I get--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was in Theresienstadt. Now I'm confused because-- because I came with-- that was-- yeah, that was immediately after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The chauffeur came with a big truck, truck. Came to pick up Mr. [? Friedman, ?] and his daughter, and myself, and took us to Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we arrived in Prague to this palace, or mansion, where Mr. Friedman lived was occupied by Russian army. We couldn't get in. So to chauffeur was a Christian. His former chauffeur. He said, why don't you come to my place, and sleep there, and see what happens, day by day? So we slept at Mr. Chauffeur's place. I forgot his name. And-- and then I was friendly for a few days with Vera, Mr.-- Mr. Friedman's daughter. But we went back to Theresienstadt to get our papers that we're free, that we're liberated, that we can show the Czech government every [? little ?] [? state ?] in Theresienstadt liberated by Russian army.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- I went back. And that's when I met Inge. OK. And that's the train there where we sat on the suitcase. And we got to Prague. And I lost her name. And I looked her up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you return to your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: My hometown, [? Levice? ?] Yes, with-- with Inge.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '45. Excuse me. I don't remember the date. But that we got to Levice. We got to Levice. And I was told-- we were told that the Jewish people were deported. Few Jewish people came back. And they told us the time when my parents were taken to Auschwitz. And there was the house there. And there was nobody there. The house was empty. And going back, some neighbors gave us some stuff to my parents gave to these neighbors to keep for me if I come back. And it was winter coat and some other stuff, suitcase. These neighbors, Christians, gave it to me. And then, with this stuff, we got to-- to Bratislava from Levice to Bratislava. And I told you the encounter with the Russian soldiers that were drunk there.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what was-- what happened then? You encountered Russian soldiers? In Bratislava.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Bratislava was occupied by Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sitting at night there waiting for trains. Trains were irregular. Waiting for the train to Prague because Inge was-- her sister lived in Prague. And she was a Prague girl, from Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened with the Russian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were drunk. And they were just, you were not safe there. You know, they-- they wanted something, and any girl-- any woman was not really very safe. And finally, the train came. It was-- it was midnight. We got into train. And the trains were-- had-- anyway. We were-- didn't-- safe-- we didn't feel safe in that train. So I told Inge, let's get out in a hurry. So we got out. And at night, then, I said, I have a friend, this Christian fellow who was an officer in the Czech army. And he married a Jewish woman. And I know where he lives. Let's find him. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Bratislava. And the town was empty. It was night. We could hear shooting, you know, the sound of gunshots. And we walked from the station to [? Palisade, ?] to [? Ali's ?] apartment building. And standing there, I yelled up, and I, [? Ali, ?] [? Ali, ?] [? Ali. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, they came to the window, opened the window. Who is this? I said, it's Albert. Oh, come into open the door. We slept there. And then what happened? What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and then we got to Prague, I guess, from Bratislava, by train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you got to Prague? Where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- I went back-- well, we went back. We-- by then, I mean, we were engaged, or at least I thought that this is it. We went back to [INAUDIBLE], where they took me out, where I was the house where I lived with the old lady there in five room apartment with the architect. Remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went back. And there was a sign on the door, taken over by the national-- by the national guard. And I knocked on the door. And an old lady came, opened the door. I said, who are you? She said-- I was-- it was taken over by the German family. So I was the cook for the Germans, she said. And if you want to come in. I said, well, we're not allowed because this is sealed off. But I'll-- I'll go back, and I'll try to see what I can-- how can I get back to the place where I was taken from.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went on the street. And lo and behold, walking there, I see a man in uniform. And I was a boxing fan. I wasn't-- I was boxing too, but not professionally.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this guy was the chief. It was the best boxer in middleweight. Gustav-- whoo, I forgot his name. Gustav. And he looked at me and said, hey, Jew, you're back? I said, Gustav. You're the champ. I was always [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] applauding for you. I always wanted you to win. And he was the champ. He said, so where--where do you want to stay? Look, see my uniform? I'm the chief for the district. He said, come with me to the office. I'll give you a piece of paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the office. He gave me a paper. Take that thing home. Go back to [INAUDIBLE]. Take it off, and you have the right to be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to the lady, take the thing off. She cooked us meals, and we stayed there. And that's where you stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- you went back to work? What did you do? What-- what did you do at that time? Were you allowed to teach again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was allowed to teach again. I became a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you become a teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the same high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was-- that was a two level high school, the one I was teaching. This was a four level high school-- high school. But I was teaching just a short while. But why? Oh yeah, then we were planning to get out to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- were you in the army at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- you see, I was-- I told you I was drafted, but I was postponed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came back, they found out-- the Czech army found out that I'm back. And they sent me a summons. And my wife was pregnant. Inge was pregnant with Paul. And they took me to number 20-- 26 infantry regiment. And I was a soldier for two months. And it was just after the war. And it was partisans in the forest. And I don't remember. It was a Hungarian town. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the partisans fighting for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The partisans were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish. I think they were Polish. They were for-- against the Polish government. But we didn't have anything to do with--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long did you stay in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was de-mobbed. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 15, 1946. And Paul was born May 28, on my wife's birthday, May 28th, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came back to Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came to Prague. And I had a friend who came back from the camps, Gus. Gus was his name. Gus [? Brill. ?] And he said, you have a big place now, a five-room place. The old lady was dead. She was gone. We were [INAUDIBLE] and my wife. And people who knew me, especially, singers, you know, David [? Garren ?] came back. He was a tenor. Beautiful voice. He-- he audition for the Metropolitan Opera later on. I accidentally turned the radio, heard his voice auditioning for the Met Opera. But he died shortly after. And Karel Berman, who became conductor of the Toronto-- of the Prague National Theater opera, he was staying with us. [? Garren ?] and Moses Singer, also Singer-- that's his name, Singer, but he was a singer. And he was, we got a piano. There was a lot of singing going on. And that's where we stayed. And this friend, Gus, told me, there is a Israeli fellow here who was buying guns for Israel. He's getting the gun out to Israel. But he was caught.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train, the car-- the train car was open, and it was declared as electrical equipment. They opened it, the customs, and they found guns there. So he escaped, but he was looking-- the radio was looking for him. Would you hide him for a few nights?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, yes. He slept there, over, with us. And when he left, because he had to sleep over some nights, over somewhere else, he said, if you need any help, look up this guy. He gave me the name. I looked up this guy. We want to get out of here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why did you want to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were all kinds of rules by the Communists, you know, that we didn't like. And I looked up this guy. I said, oh, could you help us to get out of the country? He said, yes, but it will cost you money. I said, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're gonna pause for just a moment.
